name: Mexico

colors.symbolism: originally the Catholic faith; later, unity
originally the union of Europeans and Americans; later, the blood of the national heroes

description: Three equal vertical stripes of green (hoist), white and red. Centred on the white stripe is the national coat of arms — a golden eagle, perched on a prickly-pear cactus growing from a rock in a lake, holding a writhing serpent in its beak and right talon — flanked at the base by oak and laurel branches tied with a green-white-red ribbon.
history: The green-white-red tricolour traces to the Army of the Three Guarantees (Trigarante) under Agustín de Iturbide in 1821, with the colours denoting the three guarantees of the Plan de Iguala — independence, religion, and union. The eagle-and-serpent emblem was added shortly after independence and has appeared in many forms; the present coat of arms was designed by Francisco Eppens Helguera and adopted on 16 September 1968. The current flag, with proportions 4:7 and the present coat of arms in the centre of the white stripe, was officially codified by the Law on the National Arms, Flag and Anthem of 24 February 1984.

features.symbolism: originally interpreted as independence (green), the Roman Catholic religion (white), and union of Europeans and Americans (red); reinterpreted under President Benito Juárez as hope (green), unity (white), and the blood of the national heroes (red)

depicts the Aztec foundation legend of Tenochtitlan: a golden eagle perched on a prickly-pear cactus (nopal) growing from a rock in a lake, devouring a serpent. According to the legend recorded in the Crónica Mexicáyotl, the Mexica wandered for centuries until they saw this sign at the place that became their capital. The cactus rises from a stylised island; below, an oak branch (strength) and a laurel branch (victory) tied with a ribbon in the national colours frame the lower edge.
